nativeRange.habitat: The Fine Spotted Fairy Wrasse inhabits coral reefs and rubble zones of shallow, protected reefs across its range, though it is occasionally found in more exposed areas as well. It is generally encountered at depths between 5 and 28 meters.

diet.notes: Generally advised to offer a varied diet of vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, frozen brine shrimp, and other meaty marine foods. Has been known to accept high-quality marine flake and pellet foods once acclimated. Tends to feed multiple times per day in small portions. May occasionally consume small ornamental crustaceans.

avoidWith: small ornamental shrimp (may be viewed as prey)

imageCandidates.notes: Debra Baker; 1586x1320. Vivid terminal male showing the described red/pink upper body, yellow lateral stripe, blue-green flank with fine light spotting and blue fins; sharp, whole fish framed.
Jean-Paul Boerekamps; 2048x1115. Crisp terminal male with fine turquoise spotting across the body and vivid red/purple/blue fins; different photographer and angle for variety.
Debra Baker; 1367x1090. Second vivid male with strong red/yellow/blue coloration and fine spotting; slightly busier background but colourful and in focus.

summary: The Fine Spotted Fairy Wrasse is a peaceful, reef-safe species from the Western Pacific, well suited to community reef aquariums. Males display vivid red, pink, yellow, and blue coloration with fine white spotting; females are more subdued. A tight-fitting lid is strongly advised, as this species has been known to jump.
careNotes:
The Fine Spotted Fairy Wrasse (Cirrhilabrus punctatus) is generally considered a good choice for a peaceful reef aquarium. Males tend to display more vivid coloration — typically red, pink, yellow, and blue hues with fine white spotting — while females and juveniles are somewhat duller, with a dark gray to reddish upper body and a white lower section.

This species has been reported to jump readily, and a tight-fitting canopy is strongly advised. A sand or rubble substrate with ample live rock for hiding and establishing territories tends to work well.

Like other Cirrhilabrus wrasses, this species is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning individuals are born female and the dominant fish in a group may transition to male over time. In a group setting, one male will typically pair or spawn with multiple females. Keeping a single male with one or more females is feasible in appropriately sized systems.

While the Fine Spotted Fairy Wrasse is relatively undemanding in terms of diet and temperament, the elevated escape risk and the need for stable water quality place it at an intermediate care level overall.
breedingNotes: The Fine Spotted Fairy Wrasse is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning fish are born female and the dominant individual in a group is capable of transitioning to male. Distinct pairing behavior during spawning has been observed in the wild. Captive breeding of Cirrhilabrus wrasses has been reported occasionally but is generally considered difficult and is not routine in the hobby.

fish.breedingNotes: This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite. Captive breeding is occasionally reported but is not routine in the hobby. Distinct pairing behavior during spawning has been observed in the wild.
